sections.heading: Architecture Fundamentals
sections.body:
The core abstraction of Atlas Stream Processing is the stream processor. A stream
processor is a MongoDB [aggregation pipeline](manual/core/aggregation-pipeline) that operates continuously on
streaming data from a specified source and writes the output to a
sink. To learn more, see [Structure of a Stream Processor](atlas-sp-processor-structure).

Stream processing takes place on stream processing workspaces. Each stream processing workspace is an
Atlas namespace that associates the following:

- One or more stream processors, each running on its own allocation of
  RAM and CPUs.

- A default tier, which determines the amount of memory and compute
  available to each stream processor when you don't specify a tier.

- A maximum tier, which determines the highest amount of memory and
  compute that you can allocate to a pod within that stream processing workspace.

- A cloud provider and cloud region.

- A **connection registry**, which stores the list of available
  sources and sinks of streaming data.

- A security context in which to define user authorizations.

- A [Connection String](manual/reference/connection-string) to
  the stream processing workspace itself.

sections.heading: Tiers
sections.body:
When you define a stream processor, it becomes available only for the
stream processing workspace in which you define it. Each stream processor runs on
resources allocated according to its tier. Atlas Stream Processing bills users for
a stream processor only while it's running.

If you start a stream processor without declaring a tier size, it will
run the stream processing workspace's tier. You can start a stream processor of any tier
up to and including the maximum tier of the stream processing workspace

> **Example:** You define a stream processor on a stream processing workspace named `myWorkspace`
> with a default tier of `SP10`, and a maximum tier of `SP30`. If
> you start the processor without specifying a tier, Atlas Stream Processing
> assigns it to an `SP10` pod. However, you can declare any tier
> from `SP2` up to `SP30` and Atlas Stream Processing will assign the
> processor to an appropriately sized pod.

sections.heading: Workers (Legacy)
sections.body:
Each worker can host up to four running stream processors. Stream Processing Workspaces
that run on the legacy workers model bill users according to the
number of workers. Atlas Stream Processing automatically scales your stream processing workspace up
as you start stream processors by provisioning workers as needed. You
can deprovision a worker by stopping all stream processors on
it. Atlas Stream Processing always prefers to assign a stream processor to an
existing worker over provisioning new workers.

> **Example:** You have a stream processing workspace running eight stream processors, named
> `proc01` through `proc08`. `proc01` through `proc04` run on
> one worker, `proc05` through `proc08` run on a second
> worker. You start a new stream processor named
> `proc09`. Atlas Stream Processing provisions a third worker to host
> `proc09`.
>
> Later, you stop `proc03` on the first worker. When you stop
> `proc09` and restart it, Atlas Stream Processing reassigns `proc09` to the
> first worker and deprovisions the third worker.
>
> If you start a new stream processor named `proc10` before you
> stop and restart `proc09`, Atlas Stream Processing assigns `proc10` to the
> first worker in the slot previously allocated to `proc03`.

When scaling, Atlas Stream Processing only considers the number of currently
running stream processors; it doesn't count defined stream processors
that aren't running. The tier of the stream processing workspace determines the RAM and
CPU allotment of its workers.

> **Important:** `SP10` and `SP30` processors operate and bill users
> according to the legacy worker model. These processors
> update to the per-processor pricing model on December 3rd,
> 2025\. To learn more, see the [worker model](atlas-sp-architecture-workers-legacy) section of the
> Atlas Stream Processing architecture overview.

sections.heading: Connection Registry
sections.body:
Connection registries store one or more connections. Each connection
assigns a name to the combination of networking and security details
that allow a stream processor to interact with external
services. Connections exhibit the following behavior:

- Only a connection defined in a given stream processing workspace's connection registry
  can service stream processors hosted on that stream processing workspace.

- Each connection can service an arbitrary number of stream processors

- Only a single connection can serve as a given stream processor's source.

- Only a single connection can serve as a given stream processor's sink.

- A connection is not innately defined as either a source or a sink. Any given
  connection can serve either function depending on how a stream processor
  invokes that connection.

Atlas Stream Processing runs stream processing pods in dedicated
customer containers, on multi-tenant infrastructure. For more information
on MongoDB security and compliance, see the
[MongoDB Trust Center](https://www.mongodb.com/products/platform/trust).

sections.heading: Checkpoints
sections.body:
Atlas Stream Processing captures the state of a stream processor using
checkpoints. Each checkpoint has a unique ID and are subject to the
flow of your stream processor logic. After all operators of a stream
processor add their state to a checkpoint, Atlas Stream Processing commits the
checkpoint, generating two types of records:

- A single commit record that validates the checkpoint ID and the stream
  processor to which it belongs

- A set of records describing the state of each stateful operation
  in the relevant stream processor at the instant Atlas Stream Processing
  committed the checkpoint.

When you restart a stream processor after an interruption,
Atlas Stream Processing queries the last committed checkpoint and resumes
operation from the described state.

sections.heading: Kafka Sources and Consumer Group Offsets
sections.body:
After Atlas Stream Processing commits a checkpoint, it updates the consumer group offsets
in Kafka asynchronously. When you use [Apache Kafka](https://kafka.apache.org/) as a `$source`, the consumer
groups track these checkpoints as committed offsets in the Kafka cluster for
each partition.

Because these updates happen periodically and asynchronously, the consumer
group’s committed offsets can temporarily lag behind the most recent checkpoint.
This can cause short-term delays in monitoring and lag metrics for tools that
read committed offsets from Kafka, such as the `kafka-consumer-group` CLI
tool. These tools may show the consumer group “behind” the stream processor’s
true internal position.

The amount of lag between internal checkpoints and committed Kafka offsets is
not fixed and can vary based on workload and configuration. In typical
workloads it is on the order of seconds, but there is no strict upper bound.

sections.heading: Dead Letter Queue
sections.body:
Atlas Stream Processing supports the use of an Atlas database collection as a
[dead letter queue](manual/reference/glossary) (DLQ). When Atlas Stream Processing cannot process a
document from your data stream, it writes the content of the document
to the DLQ along with details of the processing failure. You can assign
a collection as a DLQ in your stream processor definitions.

To learn more, see [Create a Stream Processor](streams-manage-create).

sections.heading: Stream Processor States
sections.body:
Stream processors transition between different states throughout their lifecycle. Understanding these states helps you monitor and troubleshoot your stream processing operations.

Possible states include:

| State        | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| ------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| RUNNING      | The stream processor is actively processing data and operating normally. This is the desired operational state where your processor consumes data from sources, applies transformations, and writes to sinks.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| STOPPED      | The stream processor has been manually stopped and is not processing data. To learn how to start a stream processor, see Start a Stream Processor.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| PROVISIONING | The stream processor is in the process of allocating resources or scaling infrastructure.Common causes include:Starting a new stream processor that requires additional worker resourcesScaling operations when existing workers are at capacityInfrastructure rebalancing during peak usage periodsAutomatic resource provisioning triggered by workload demandsStream Processing Workspace tier changes requiring resource reallocationPotential impacts include:Temporary delay in stream processor startup (typically 2-5 minutes)Brief interruption in data processing during transitionsOnce provisioning completes, processes resume automatically. No data loss occurs, as Atlas Stream Processing preserves state through checkpoints. |
| FAILED       | The stream processor has encountered an error that prevents it from running. This occurs due to user errors or configuration issues. For detailed information about failure causes and recovery, see Error Handling and Retry Policies.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |

> **Note:** Stream processors might appear intermittently in PROVISIONING state in monitoring dashboards as the system allocates resources. This is normal behavior during scaling operations. If a processor remains in PROVISIONING state for extended periods (>10 minutes), check your stream processing workspace resource limits and tier configuration.

sections.heading: Error Handling and Retry Policies
sections.body:
Atlas Stream Processing implements comprehensive error handling and retry policies to
ensure reliable stream processing. The system distinguishes between different
types of errors and applies appropriate retry strategies based on the error
classification.

sections.heading: Error Classification
sections.body:
Atlas Stream Processing classifies errors into two main categories:

**User Errors**

Errors caused by user configuration, data issues, or external service
problems that are outside of Atlas Stream Processing's control. Examples include:

- Invalid connection credentials

- Network connectivity issues to external services

- Malformed data that cannot be processed

- Permission issues accessing external resources

**Internal Errors**

Errors that occur within the Atlas Stream Processing system itself, typically due to
temporary infrastructure issues or service disruptions. These are considered
the responsibility of the Atlas Stream Processing service to resolve.

sections.heading: Retry Behavior
sections.body:
The retry behavior varies based on the error classification:

**User Error Retry Policy**

- Atlas Stream Processing attempts to restart the stream processor a limited number of
  times over a 5-minute period

- If all retry attempts fail within this timeframe, the stream processor
  transitions to a `FAILED` state

- Some user errors are classified as non-retryable and immediately cause
  the processor to fail. Examples include:

  - `StreamProcessorWorkerOutOfMemory (418)`: Pipeline exceeds tier memory
    limits

  - `StreamProcessorInvalidOptions (420)`: Invalid pipeline syntax or
    configuration

- You can manually restart a failed stream processor by calling the
  `start()` method

**Internal Error Retry Policy**

- Atlas Stream Processing continuously retries internal errors without a time limit

- Internal errors trigger alerts to the Atlas Stream Processing engineering team

- The system relies on automatic retry mechanisms to recover processors
  after internal issues are resolved

- Stream processors automatically resume from their last
  [checkpoints](atlas-sp-checkpoints) when restarted

sections.heading: Recovery Process
sections.body:
When a stream processor encounters an error and requires a restart, the
recovery process follows these steps:

sections.heading: Step 1: State preservation
sections.body:
The current state is preserved through the
[checkpoints](atlas-sp-checkpoints) mechanism.

sections.heading: Step 2: Restart attempt
sections.body:
Atlas Stream Processing attempts to restart the processor
according to the applicable retry policy.

sections.heading: Step 3: State recovery
sections.body:
Upon successful restart, the processor resumes
operation from the last committed checkpoint.

sections.heading: Step 4: Continued processing
sections.body:
The processor continues processing from where
it left off, ensuring no data loss.

This recovery process ensures that temporary failures don't result in data
loss or require manual intervention in most cases.

sections.heading: Stream Processing Timing
sections.body:
In streaming data processing, documents are subject to two timing
systems:

- event time

- processing time

Atlas Stream Processing offers various parameters to control how stream
processors interact with these timing systems.

sections.heading: Event Time
sections.body:
Event time is the time at which either the source stream generates a
document, or the messaging system (e.g. [Apache Kafka](https://kafka.apache.org/)) receives the document.
This is ascertained by the timestamp of the document.

Network latency, upstream processing, and other factors can not only
cause discrepancies between these times for a given document, but can
also cause documents to arrive in a stream processor out of event-time
order. In either case, windows can miss documents that you intend for
them to capture. Atlas Stream Processing considers such documents
**late-arriving**, and sends them to your [Dead Letter Queue](atlas-sp-dlq) if you configure one.

**Event Time** is a configurable option for the
`boundary` field supported in [Tumbling Windows](tumbling-windows)
and [Hopping Windows](hopping-windows).

sections.heading: Processing Time
sections.body:
Processing time is the time at which the stream processor consumes a
document. This is ascertained by the clock of the system hosting the
stream processor.

**Processing Time** is a configurable option for the `boundary`
field supported in [Tumbling Windows](tumbling-windows) and
[Hopping Windows](hopping-windows). It allows you to create a
pipeline with a kind of window which accumulates data based on the
wall clock time of the server. As opposed to [event time](atlas-sp-event-time) windows, processing time windows assign each
event a timestamp based on the wall clock time of the server when it
arrives at the stream processor.

Document timestamps and window boundary timestamps are in UTC (Coordinated Universal Time).  You
can't specify [idleTimeout](atlas-sp-idle-timeout) or
[allowedLateness](atlas-sp-allowed-late) options when
configuring a `processingTime` window.

> **Example:** You create a pipeline with a 5 minute event time window. An event is added
> to a source Kafka cluster at `09:33`. Due to some delay in the Kafka
> cluster, it arrives at the stream processor at `09:37`.
>
> If the pipeline has a 5 minute event time window, then this event will be
> assigned to the `09:30-09:35` window. If the pipeline has a 5 minute
> processing time window, then the event will instead be assigned to the
> `09:35-09:40` window.

sections.heading: Watermarks
sections.body:
A watermark supersedes [Processing Time](atlas-sp-processing-time) and
updates only when the processor consumes a document with a later event time
than any previously-consumed document. All stream processors apply watermarks
in Atlas Stream Processing.

sections.heading: Example
sections.body:
You configure a stream processor with 5-minute windows. You start the
processor at `12:00`, so that the first two windows will have
durations of `12:00-12:05` and `12:05-12:10`. The following table
illustrates which windows will capture which events given varying
delays, with and without watermarks.

| Event Time | Processing Time | Window Time (No Watermarks) | Window Time (Watermarks) |
| ---------- | --------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| 12:00      | 12:00           | 12:00-12:05                 | 12:00-12:05              |
| 12:01      | 12:03           | 12:00-12:05                 | 12:00-12:05              |
| 12:02      | 12:05           | 12:05-12:10                 | 12:00-12:05              |
| 12:01      | 12:06           | 12:05-12:10                 | 12:00-12:05              |
| 12:06      | 12:07           | 12:05-12:10                 | 12:05-12:10              |

Without watermarks, the `12:00-12:05` window closes at `12:05`
according to the system clock of the stream processing workspace, and the `12:05-12:10`
window opens immediately. Thus, though the source generated
four of the documents during the `12:00-12:05` interval, the
relevant window captures only two documents.

With watermarks, the `12:00-12:05` window doesn't close at `12:05`
because among the documents it ingests up to that point, the latest
event time—and thus the watermark value—is `12:03`. The
`12:00-12:05` window doesn't close until `12:07` on the system
clock, when the stream processor ingests a document with an event time
of `12:05`, advances the watermark to that time, and opens the
`12:05-12:10` window. Each window captures all of the appropriate
documents.

When reading from [Apache Kafka](https://kafka.apache.org/), Atlas Stream Processing waits for all partitions to
pass the watermark.  If a partition idles and fails to produce events
with timestamps later than the watermark, the window doesn't close or
output results.  To address this, set `partitionIdleTimeout` to
ensure that idle partitions don't halt the progression of
watermarks. To learn more, see [aggregation pipelines](atlas-sp-agg-source).

sections.heading: Allowed Lateness
sections.body:
If the differences between event time and processing time vary
sufficiently, documents may arrive in a stream processor after the
watermark has advanced enough to close the expected window. To
mitigate this, Atlas Stream Processing supports Allowed Lateness, a setting which
delays a window closing by a set interval relative to the watermark.

While watermarks are properties of stream processors, Allowed Lateness
is a property of a window, and only affects when that window
closes. If the stream processor's watermark advances to a point that
would trigger a new window to open, Allowed Lateness keeps earlier
windows open without preventing this.

sections.heading: Example
sections.body:
You configure a stream processor with 5-minute tumbling windows. You
start the processor at `12:00`, so that the first two windows will
have durations of `12:00-12:05` and `12:05-12:10`. You set an
Allowed Lateness of 2 minutes.

The table below reflects the order in which the stream processor
ingests the described documents.

| Event Time | Watermark | Allowed Lateness Time | Window Time              |
| ---------- | --------- | --------------------- | ------------------------ |
| 12:00      | 12:00     | 11:58                 | 12:00-12:05              |
| 12:02      | 12:03     | 12:01                 | 12:00-12:05              |
| 12:01      | 12:04     | 12:02                 | 12:00-12:05              |
| 12:05      | 12:05     | 12:03                 | 12:00-12:15, 12:05-12:10 |
| 12:04      | 12:06     | 12:04                 | 12:00-12:05, 12:05-12:10 |
| 12:07      | 12:07     | 12:05                 | 12:05-12:10              |

When the watermark advances to `12:05`, the `12:05-12:10` window
opens. However, because the Allowed Lateness interval is 2 minutes,
from within the `12:00-12:05` window, it is effectively only
`12:03`, so it stays open. Only when the watermark advances to
`12:07` does the adjusted time reach `12:05`. At this point, the
`12:00-12:05` window closes.

sections.heading: Idleness Timeout
sections.body:
Decoupling windowing behavior from processing time by default improves
stream processing correctness in most cases. However, a streaming data
source may have periods of extended idleness. In this scenario, a
window may capture events prior to the period of idleness and be unable
to return processed results while waiting for the watermark to advance
enough to close.

Atlas Stream Processing allows users to configure an idleness timeout for windows
to mitigate these scenarios using processing time. An idleness timeout
is an interval that begins when processing time passes the end of an
open window's interval, and the stream processor's source is idle. If
the source remains idle for an interval equal to the idleness timeout,
the window closes and the watermark advances independent of any
document ingestion.

sections.heading: Example
sections.body:
You configure a tumbling window with a 3-minute interval and a
1-minute idleness timeout. The following table illustrates the effects
of the idleness timeout during and after a window's interval.

| Processing Time | Event Time or Status | Watermark | Window Time |
| --------------- | -------------------- | --------- | ----------- |
| 12:00           | 12:00                | 12:00     | 12:00-12:03 |
| 12:01           | Source idle          | 12:00     | 12:00-12:03 |
| 12:02           | Source idle          | 12:00     | 12:00-12:03 |
| 12:03           | Source idle          | 12:00     | 12:00-12:03 |
| 12:04           | 12:02                | 12:02     | 12:00-12:03 |
| 12:05           | 12:05                | 12:05     | 12:03-12:06 |
| 12:06           | Source idle          | 12:05     | 12:03-12:06 |
| 12:07           | Source idle          | 12:00     | 12:06-12:09 |
| 12:08           | Source idle          | 12:00     | 12:06-12:09 |
| 12:09           | 12:09                | 12:09     | 12:09-12:12 |

During the `12:00-12:03` interval, the source idles for three
minutes, but the stream processor doesn't close the window because
processing time is not past the end of the window's interval, and the
source does not remain idle after the window's interval ends. When the
watermark advances to `12:05`, the window closes normally, and the
`12:03-12:06` window opens.

When the source goes idle at `12:06`, it remains idle through
`12:07`, triggering the idleness timeout and advancing the watermark
to `12:06`.
